case_studies.title: Recognition and Emergency Management of Suspected Tension Pneumothorax in a Pediatric Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy presents to a rural primary health center with sudden worsening of respiratory distress after a week of fever and cough. He was previously diagnosed with pneumonia and started on oral antibiotics. His mother reports that he became acutely breathless and increasingly drowsy during the last hour. On examination, he is tachypneic (RR 48/min), tachycardic (HR 160/min), and hypotensive (BP 78/50 mmHg). SpO2 is 84% on room air. He appears cyanotic, with cool peripheries and delayed capillary refill (>4 seconds). Auscultation reveals markedly reduced air entry over the left hemithorax with hyperresonance on percussion. The right lung fields have vesicular breath sounds. No chest radiograph is available at the facility. The staff notes progressive bradycardia during the evaluation. The nearest referral hospital is 30 km away, requiring at least 45 minutes for transfer.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the clinical presentation of tension pneumothorax in a child with acute deterioration and understand the urgency of needle decompression in unstable cases.

case_studies.title: Recognition and Initial Management of Tension Pneumothorax in a Pediatric Emergency
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a rural emergency center in India with sudden-onset worsening respiratory distress. He has a history of fever and cough for 3 days, initially managed with oral antibiotics. On arrival, he is tachypneic with a respiratory rate of 48/min, oxygen saturation of 84% on room air, and a heart rate of 142/min. His blood pressure is 88/54 mmHg, and he appears pale and drowsy. Focused examination reveals significant subcostal and intercostal retractions, absent breath sounds on the left side, and hyperresonance to percussion on the same side. The right lung has diminished air entry with no added sounds. Capillary refill time is prolonged at 4 seconds, and his extremities are cool. Despite supplemental oxygen via a non-rebreather mask, there is no improvement in oxygen saturation. The rural center lacks bedside ultrasound and portable radiography. The on-call physician considers whether immediate intervention is required before arranging transfer to a higher center for definitive care.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the clinical features of tension pneumothorax in a child and understand the indications for immediate needle decompression in an unstable patient.

case_studies.title: Recognition and Initial Management of Tension Pneumothorax in a Pediatric Emergency
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy presents to a district hospital in rural India with a 2-day history of fever and cough. He suddenly develops severe respiratory distress in the emergency room, with marked tachypnea, nasal flaring, and use of accessory muscles. His oxygen saturation drops to 82% on room air, and he becomes increasingly agitated. On examination, his chest shows asymmetrical movement with decreased air entry and hyperresonance on the right side. His heart rate is 160 bpm, blood pressure is 84/56 mmHg, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. The child becomes drowsy, with a weak pulse and narrowing pulse pressure. A portable chest X-ray is unavailable, and the facility lacks bedside ultrasound. The attending physician suspects a rapidly deteriorating condition and considers immediate intervention while planning for transfer to a higher center for definitive care.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and promptly initiate management for tension pneumothorax in a child presenting with sudden respiratory decompensation and signs of obstructive shock.

case_studies.title: Sudden Respiratory Distress in a Mechanically Ventilated Child
case_studies.vignette: A 5-year-old boy with a history of severe pneumonia is admitted to a district hospital and is on mechanical ventilation. On day 3 of admission, the nurse notes a sudden drop in SpO2 from 96% to 78%, accompanied by rising peak airway pressures on the ventilator. The child appears increasingly tachycardic (HR 160 bpm), with cool extremities and delayed capillary refill. On examination, there is significant respiratory distress, with markedly reduced air entry on the right side and hyperresonance to percussion over the same area. The child’s blood pressure is 84/50 mmHg, and the pulse pressure is narrow. No chest radiograph is immediately available due to equipment failure. The treating physician is concerned about rapid decompensation and considers immediate intervention. The family is informed of the need for possible transfer to a higher center for further care after stabilization.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and promptly intervene in a suspected tension pneumothorax in an unstable child, while identifying criteria for transfer to a higher-level facility.

case_studies.title: Pediatric Tension Pneumothorax in a Resource-Limited Setting
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy presents to a rural primary health center in India with sudden onset of severe respiratory distress. He was previously admitted for treatment of severe pneumonia and discharged a week ago. His mother reports that he was improving until this morning when he developed rapid breathing, irritability, and refusal to eat. On examination, he is in severe respiratory distress with nasal flaring and intercostal retractions. His heart rate is 160 bpm, respiratory rate is 50/min, and oxygen saturation is 82% on room air. He appears pale with cool extremities and a capillary refill time of 4 seconds. Breath sounds are markedly diminished on the left side, and there is hyperresonance to percussion over the same area. No chest wall deformities or external trauma are noted. The nearest district hospital is 50 km away, with no ambulance readily available. A pulse oximeter shows no improvement in oxygen saturation despite high-flow oxygen via face mask. The child’s condition appears to be worsening, with increasing lethargy and a narrowing pulse pressure.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the clinical signs of a tension pneumothorax in an unstable child and understand the need for immediate needle decompression and subsequent chest drain placement.

mcqs.stem: After needle decompression for suspected tension pneumothorax in a 6-year-old, what is the next priority?
mcqs.explanation: Needle decompression is a temporary measure. Definitive management requires chest drain placement to prevent recurrence and ensure continuous evacuation of air.

choices.rationale: Incorrect: Transfer without placing a chest drain risks recurrence during transport.
Incorrect: Repeat decompression is unnecessary if the initial needle decompression was effective.
Correct: A chest drain is required for definitive management of pneumothorax after needle decompression.
Incorrect: IV fluids may support perfusion but do not address the underlying pneumothorax.

mcqs.explanation: The child shows signs of tension pneumothorax, including severe respiratory distress, unilateral findings, and obstructive shock physiology. Immediate needle decompression is life-saving and should not be delayed.

choices.rationale: Correct: Needle decompression is the first step for suspected tension pneumothorax in an unstable child.

subtopics.subtopic_title: INTRAMUSCULAR EPINEPHRINE DOSING FLUID RESUSCITATION AND OBSERVATION DECISIONS
subtopics.category: ANAPHYLAXIS EMERGENCY RECOGNITION AND EPINEPHRINE THERAPY
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Anaphylaxis is a clinical diagnosis and should not be delayed for confirmation of hypotension or skin findings. It is characterized by acute onset of airway or breathing compromise (e.g., stridor, wheeze, hypoxemia), circulation compromise (e.g., collapse, weak pulses), or severe gastrointestinal symptoms following exposure to a likely trigger. Intramuscular (IM) epinephrine is the first-line treatment and must be administered immediately. The recommended dose is 0.01 mg/kg of 1 mg/mL (1:1000) solution, injected into the mid-anterolateral thigh, with a maximum dose of 0.5 mg, repeated every 5–15 minutes if symptoms persist.
- **Clinical application:** Place the child in a supine position with legs elevated unless breathing is easier in a sitting position. Administer high-flow oxygen, establish IV access, and provide isotonic fluid boluses (e.g., 20 mL/kg) for shock, with reassessment guiding repeated doses. Adjunct therapies such as inhaled salbutamol for bronchospasm, antihistamines for urticaria, and corticosteroids for persistent symptoms can be used but must not replace epinephrine. Escalate to an epinephrine infusion in cases of refractory shock or when repeated IM doses are required, and ensure urgent transfer to a facility capable of continuous monitoring.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Observe all children for at least 4 hours after the last epinephrine dose due to the risk of biphasic reactions. Prolonged monitoring or admission is indicated for severe respiratory compromise, hypotension, multiple epinephrine doses, comorbid asthma, or limited access to emergency care. In India, discharge counseling should emphasize trigger avoidance, provision of a written emergency plan, and ensuring access to epinephrine, either via auto-injectors or prefilled syringe strategies where policy permits. These measures are critical to reducing recurrence and improving outcomes.

case_studies.title: Anaphylaxis in a 6-Year-Old Following Peanut Ingestion
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department of a district hospital in rural India by his parents 20 minutes after eating a sweet at a family gathering. He immediately developed swelling of his lips and tongue, followed by difficulty breathing and vomiting. On examination, he is visibly distressed with audible stridor and wheezing. His respiratory rate is 36 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation is 88% on room air, and his heart rate is 140 beats per minute. Peripheral pulses are weak, and his extremities are cool. His blood pressure is 78/50 mmHg. There is generalized urticaria on his trunk and arms. His parents report no prior history of allergies but note that the sweet contained peanuts, which he had never eaten before. The facility has limited resources, and the nearest tertiary care center is 2 hours away by road.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize anaphylaxis as a clinical diagnosis, initiate immediate IM epinephrine, and determine the need for fluid resuscitation and observation based on severity and resource limitations.

case_studies.title: Anaphylaxis in a School-Aged Child Following Peanut Ingestion
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents after developing sudden difficulty breathing and vomiting at school. He had eaten a peanut-based snack 20 minutes earlier, despite a known peanut allergy. On arrival, he appears anxious, is gasping for air, and has audible wheezing. His lips are swollen, and he has widespread urticarial rash over his trunk and arms. His heart rate is 140 bpm, respiratory rate 34/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, and oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. Peripheral pulses are weak, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. His parents report that he had a similar reaction a year ago, which resolved after treatment with an injection at a local clinic. The school teacher provided no treatment en route to the hospital. The emergency department is in a semi-urban Indian setting with limited pediatric critical care resources. The child is placed on a stretcher, and the staff is preparing to initiate treatment.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize anaphylaxis as a clinical diagnosis and prioritize immediate administration of intramuscular epinephrine, fluid resuscitation, and monitoring decisions in a resource-limited setting.

case_studies.title: Anaphylaxis in a 6-Year-Old After Peanut Ingestion
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy is brought to a rural primary health center in India by his parents 20 minutes after eating a sweet at a family event. Shortly after ingestion, he developed lip swelling, generalized itching, and abdominal pain, followed by difficulty breathing and a brief episode of vomiting. On arrival, he appears anxious and is sitting upright, struggling to breathe. His respiratory rate is 40/min with audible wheeze, and oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. His heart rate is 140/min, and his blood pressure is 84/50 mmHg. Examination reveals diffuse urticaria, nasal flaring, and accessory muscle use. Peripheral pulses are weak, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. No prior history of allergies is reported. The nearest tertiary hospital is 45 minutes away by road.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize anaphylaxis as a clinical diagnosis and initiate immediate intramuscular epinephrine, fluid resuscitation, and supportive care, while determining observation and transfer needs.

case_studies.title: Anaphylaxis Following Peanut Ingestion in a 6-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy is brought to a rural primary health center in India by his parents 20 minutes after eating a piece of homemade peanut laddoo. He developed sudden lip swelling, generalized hives, and vomiting within minutes of ingestion. On arrival, he is anxious, tachypneic, and complaining of difficulty breathing. His respiratory rate is 36/min with audible wheeze, oxygen saturation is 88% on room air, and his heart rate is 140/min. Peripheral pulses are weak, and his capillary refill time is 4 seconds. His blood pressure is not immediately available due to equipment limitations. The parents report no prior history of food allergies or asthma. The facility is equipped with basic emergency supplies, including oxygen, epinephrine, and IV fluids, but lacks advanced monitoring capabilities. The boy’s symptoms appear to be worsening, and his parents are visibly distressed.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and initiate immediate treatment for anaphylaxis, including intramuscular epinephrine dosing, fluid resuscitation, and decisions regarding observation or transfer in a resource-limited setting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: EARLY SHOCK RECOGNITION FLUID TITRATION AND VASOACTIVE SELECTION IN CHILDREN
subtopics.category: SHOCK SEPSIS AND DENGUE HEMODYNAMIC EMERGENCIES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Shock in children is a clinical diagnosis that often precedes hypotension. Key signs include tachycardia disproportionate to fever, cool extremities, weak pulses, prolonged capillary refill, altered sensorium, and reduced urine output. Hemodynamic patterns should be classified when possible: cold shock (low cardiac output with high systemic vascular resistance) is more common in pediatric sepsis, while warm shock (bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure) may occur early.
- **Clinical application:** Initial fluid management involves isotonic crystalloid boluses of 10–20 mL/kg over 10–15 minutes, with frequent reassessment of lung exam, liver size, capillary refill, and mental status. Boluses should be slowed or stopped if signs of fluid overload such as respiratory distress, crackles, or hepatomegaly develop, and vasoactive support should be initiated earlier in such cases. For cold shock with weak pulses and narrow pulse pressure, epinephrine is preferred, while norepinephrine is appropriate for warm shock with bounding pulses and low diastolic pressure. Concurrently, address underlying causes such as hemorrhage, dehydration, tension pneumothorax, hypoglycemia, and suspected sepsis with early antibiotics.
- **Safety/follow-up:** In resource-limited settings, repeated focused bedside assessments are safer than fixed-volume protocols for fluid titration. Peripheral infusion of vasoactives can be cautiously used while arranging central access if shock persists after initial fluids. In India, early referral is critical when vasoactives are needed due to risks of sudden decompensation and limited PICU availability. Timely escalation and transport to higher centers are essential to reduce mortality.

questions.stem: After administering a 20 mL/kg fluid bolus to a child in cold shock, he remains drowsy with weak pulses and prolonged capillary refill. What is the next best step?
questions.explanation: If shock persists after the first bolus and there are no signs of fluid overload (e.g., hepatomegaly, crackles), a second fluid bolus is indicated before considering vasoactive agents.

choices.rationale: Incorrect: Maintenance fluids do not address ongoing shock and are not appropriate in this setting.
Incorrect: Referral without further interventions risks worsening shock during transport.
Correct: A second fluid bolus is appropriate if there are no signs of fluid overload and the child remains in shock.
Incorrect: Epinephrine is considered after fluid resuscitation fails or is contraindicated due to fluid overload.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child presents with tachycardia, cool extremities, prolonged capillary refill, and weak pulses. Which type of shock is most likely?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Cold shock.' It is characterized by cool extremities, weak pulses, and prolonged capillary refill due to low cardiac output and high systemic vascular resistance.

choices.rationale: Incorrect. Warm shock typically presents with bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure, not cool extremities or weak pulses.
Incorrect. Obstructive shock has distinct causes like tension pneumothorax, which are not described here.
Incorrect. Cardiogenic shock may present with similar signs but is not specified as the most common type in pediatric sepsis.
Correct. Cold shock presents with cool extremities, prolonged capillary refill, and weak pulses due to low cardiac output and high systemic vascular resistance.

case_studies.title: Early Recognition and Management of Shock in a Febrile Child
case_studies.vignette: A 4-year-old boy is brought to a rural primary health center in India with a 2-day history of high-grade fever, lethargy, and decreased oral intake. His parents report that he has passed only a small amount of urine in the last 12 hours and has become increasingly drowsy. On examination, his temperature is 39.2°C, heart rate is 160/min, respiratory rate is 36/min, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. His extremities are cold and mottled, with weak peripheral pulses. He is minimally responsive to stimuli. There is no significant respiratory distress, and his lungs are clear on auscultation. The abdomen reveals mild hepatomegaly. A bedside glucose test shows a random blood sugar of 90 mg/dL. The facility has limited resources, including only basic intravenous fluids and no advanced monitoring equipment. The nearest higher center is 3 hours away by road.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize early signs of shock in a febrile child, classify the hemodynamic pattern, and apply principles of fluid titration and early consideration of vasoactive support in a resource-limited setting.

Correct: Initial fluid resuscitation with 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid is the standard for cold shock. Reassess frequently.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SEPSIS ANTIBIOTIC BUNDLE TIMING CULTURE COLLECTION AND DEESCALATION STRATEGY
subtopics.category: SHOCK SEPSIS AND DENGUE HEMODYNAMIC EMERGENCIES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** In suspected pediatric sepsis, timely administration of antibiotics within 1 hour is critical, especially in cases of septic shock. Blood cultures should be obtained prior to antibiotic administration if this does not delay treatment. Empiric antibiotic selection should consider the likely source of infection, patient age, prior antibiotic exposure, and local antibiogram data. In India, antimicrobial stewardship is essential due to high resistance rates and variable diagnostic reliability.
- **Clinical application:** For unstable neonates, Indian protocols commonly recommend ampicillin (or cloxacillin) 50 mg/kg per dose plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg per dose, with ampicillin dosing adjusted by postnatal age and weight. Routine use of third-generation cephalosporins is discouraged unless meningitis is suspected, as they increase the risk of ESBL selection and invasive fungal infections. For immunized children hospitalized with uncomplicated community-acquired pneumonia, ampicillin 50 mg/kg per dose (max 2 g) IV every 6 hours is standard, with step-down to oral amoxicillin upon stabilization. In suspected meningitis, ceftriaxone-based regimens are preferred, with vancomycin added if resistant pneumococcus is a concern; antibiotics should not be delayed for lumbar puncture or imaging in unstable children.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Reassess the clinical picture and microbiology findings at 48–72 hours rather than relying solely on inflammatory markers. If cultures are negative and the child is improving, consider narrowing or stopping antibiotics and evaluating for viral illnesses, malaria, dengue, or noninfectious mimics. When a pathogen is identified, de-escalate to the narrowest effective agent and define antibiotic duration based on the syndrome and clinical response. Avoid unnecessary 'antibiotic stacking,' as structured de-escalation minimizes adverse effects and preserves antibiotic efficacy.

case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to a rural primary health center in India with a 2-day history of fever, irritability, and poor feeding. The parents report no history of cough, rash, or diarrhea. The child was born at term, vaccinated as per schedule, and has no significant past medical history. On examination, he is lethargic with a temperature of 39.2°C, heart rate of 170 bpm, respiratory rate of 50/min, and capillary refill time of 4 seconds. The facility has limited resources, but a blood culture can be sent to a nearby lab. The treating physician considers initiating empiric antibiotics immediately. A decision must also be made about obtaining cultures before starting treatment, balancing the urgency of the situation. The parents are anxious and inquire about the duration of hospitalization and treatment.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SEVERE MALARIA RECOGNITION ARTESUNATE DOSING AND FIRST HOUR COINTERVENTIONS
subtopics.category: SHOCK SEPSIS AND DENGUE HEMODYNAMIC EMERGENCIES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Severe malaria should be a top differential diagnosis in febrile Indian children with rapid clinical deterioration, especially if they present with impaired consciousness, repeated seizures, respiratory distress, hypoglycemia, severe anemia, shock, jaundice with organ dysfunction, hemoglobinuria, or oliguria. Misdiagnosing these cases as 'viral fever' or 'dengue' can delay life-saving parenteral antimalarial therapy. Early recognition and intervention are critical to prevent progression to life-threatening complications.
- **Clinical application:** Stabilize the child using the ABCDE approach while addressing coexisting threats such as hypoglycemia, seizures, and shock. Check bedside glucose immediately and correct hypoglycemia, as it exacerbates encephalopathy and seizures in cerebral malaria. Treat seizures with benzodiazepines per protocol, alongside aggressive correction of glucose and temperature. For shock, administer small isotonic fluid aliquots with frequent reassessment, avoiding repeated large boluses due to the risk of mixed shock physiology and severe anemia in malaria.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Start injectable artesunate immediately when clinical severe malaria criteria are met, without waiting for smear or RDT confirmation. Follow the Indian dosing guideline: 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12, and 24 hours, then once daily, transitioning to a 3-day oral ACT course when the child can tolerate oral medications. Severe anemia (hemoglobin <5 g/dL or hematocrit <20%) with respiratory distress, hypoxia, or heart failure signs mandates early blood transfusion. Escalate care and consider transfer for persistent altered sensorium, recurrent seizures, escalating oxygen or ventilation needs, shock requiring repeated boluses or vasoactives, renal failure with oliguria, severe acidosis, or recurrent hypoglycemia.

case_studies.title: Severe Malaria with Rapid Deterioration
case_studies.vignette: A 5-year-old boy from a rural village in central India is brought to the emergency department in the evening with high-grade fever for 3 days, vomiting, and increasing lethargy over the past 6 hours. His parents report that he had two episodes of generalized seizures en route to the hospital. On examination, he is somnolent with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 10/15 (E3V2M5). His temperature is 39.5°C, heart rate 140/min, respiratory rate 36/min with mild subcostal retractions, and blood pressure 70/45 mmHg. His capillary refill time is 4 seconds, and his peripheral pulses are weak. He is pale and icteric, with cold extremities. Bedside random blood glucose is 38 mg/dL. No rash or significant lymphadenopathy is noted. A point-of-care hemoglobin check shows 4.8 g/dL.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and prioritize severe malaria in febrile children with rapid deterioration and initiate urgent interventions including artesunate dosing, glucose correction, seizure management, and stabilization of shock.

His parents report that he became progressively drowsy over the past 6 hours and had one episode of generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting about 2 minutes before arrival. On examination, he is febrile (39.5°C), lethargic, and does not respond to verbal commands. His respiratory rate is 38/min with mild intercostal retractions, and oxygen saturation is 91% on room air. His heart rate is 140/min, capillary refill time is 4 seconds, and blood pressure is 70/40 mmHg. He has pale conjunctiva and a weak peripheral pulse. Bedside glucose is 45 mg/dL. subtopics.subtopic_title: DENGUE WARNING SIGN RECOGNITION AND FLUID MANAGEMENT DURING THE CRITICAL PHASE
subtopics.category: SHOCK SEPSIS AND DENGUE HEMODYNAMIC EMERGENCIES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Dengue in children often worsens during the critical phase, typically days 3–7, around defervescence. Warning signs include persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain, mucosal bleeding, lethargy/restlessness, postural dizziness, hepatomegaly, rising hematocrit with falling platelets, or shock features. Plasma leakage is a key pathophysiological concern, and triage decisions should prioritize early recognition of these signs.
- **Clinical application:** Fluid management during the critical phase must balance perfusion restoration and avoidance of fluid overload. For compensated dengue shock, administer 10 mL/kg crystalloid over 1 hour, tapering to maintenance as the child improves. In hypotensive shock, use 20 mL/kg crystalloid over 15–30 minutes with immediate reassessment. Rising hematocrit with shock indicates plasma leakage, requiring additional crystalloid or colloid, while falling hematocrit with shock suggests bleeding and necessitates blood transfusion. Platelet transfusion should be reserved for significant bleeding or hemodynamic instability, not solely based on platelet count.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Avoid NSAIDs and intramuscular injections; use paracetamol for fever and ensure hydration. Monitor for fluid overload signs such as crackles, gallop rhythm, or increasing oxygen requirement, especially during the recovery phase when capillary leakage resolves. Document fluid rates and reassessment times meticulously to prevent errors during shift changes. Frequent reassessment of pulse volume, capillary refill, respiratory rate, urine output, and mental status is critical for timely intervention.

references.excerpt: MoHFW and NCVBDC: National Guidelines for Clinical Management of Dengue Fever (2023) — https://ncvbdc.mohfw.gov.in/WriteReadData/l892s/42616651361605607948.pdf
IAP Standard Treatment Guidelines: Dengue — https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-012-Dengue.pdf
IAP Standard Treatment Guidelines: Shock in office practice (dengue shock physiology) — https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-073-Shock-in-Office-Practice.pdf
WHO: Dengue guidelines for diagnosis, treatment, prevention and control (2009) — https://iris.who.int/handle/10665/44188

questions.stem: A 10-year-old with dengue shock has a rising hematocrit and cold extremities. What is the best initial fluid management?
questions.explanation: 20 mL/kg crystalloid over 15–30 minutes is the correct initial fluid management for hypotensive dengue shock with rising hematocrit, indicating plasma leakage.

choices.rationale: NSAIDs are contraindicated in dengue due to bleeding risk.
Platelet transfusion is reserved for significant bleeding or instability, not for shock with rising hematocrit.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DENGUE FLUID RATE TITRATION HEMATOCRIT GUIDED TRANSFUSION AND RECOVERY PHASE STOP POINTS
subtopics.category: SHOCK SEPSIS AND DENGUE HEMODYNAMIC EMERGENCIES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Dengue can rapidly deteriorate during the critical phase, typically days 3–7, especially around defervescence. Key warning signs include persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain, mucosal bleeding, lethargy or restlessness, postural dizziness, hepatomegaly, rising hematocrit with falling platelets, or any features of shock. Admission or referral is mandatory when these signs are present. Avoid NSAIDs and intramuscular injections; use paracetamol for fever and maintain enteral hydration when feasible.
- **Clinical application:** In the critical phase, fluid overload from unstructured boluses is a major preventable harm. Use isotonic crystalloids and titrate to the smallest volume that restores perfusion, guided by frequent reassessment of pulse volume, capillary refill, respiratory effort, urine output, and mental status. For dengue with warning signs but no hypotension, initiate IV crystalloid at 7–10 mL/kg/hour for 1 hour, then taper stepwise (e.g., 5–7 mL/kg/hour for 1–2 hours, 3–5 mL/kg/hour for 2–4 hours, and 2–3 mL/kg/hour for 2–4 hours) as clinical improvement occurs. Discontinue IV fluids by 24–48 hours as the recovery phase begins and capillary leak resolves.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Hematocrit trends are critical for guiding escalation in shock. Rising hematocrit with shock indicates ongoing plasma leakage and may require additional crystalloid or colloid, while falling hematocrit with shock suggests significant bleeding, necessitating blood transfusion rather than more crystalloid. Platelet count alone should not trigger transfusion. Escalate promptly for inotropes, ventilation, or transfer if hypotension persists despite appropriate fluid titration or if signs of fluid overload develop.

case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old boy from a semi-urban area presents to the emergency department on day 5 of fever. His parents report that he has been lethargic and refusing food for the past 12 hours. He has had three episodes of vomiting since morning and complains of severe abdominal pain. On examination, he appears drowsy but arousable, with a pulse rate of 120/min, capillary refill time of 3 seconds, and cool extremities. His blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. He has mild hepatomegaly and no overt bleeding, but petechiae are noted on his arms. Initial laboratory results show a hematocrit of 48% (baseline 38%) and a platelet count of 28,000/μL. His urine output has decreased in the last 8 hours. The fever subsided earlier today, but his symptoms have worsened since then.

His initial hematocrit is 48% (baseline 38% from 2 days ago), and his platelet count is 45,000/µL. Urine output has been minimal over the past 6 hours. No active mucosal bleeding is noted. The family reports they have been giving him oral fluids, but he has been unable to tolerate much due to vomiting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION MODIFICATIONS IN SHOCK SEPSIS AND DEHYDRATION
subtopics.category: SHOCK SEPSIS AND DENGUE HEMODYNAMIC EMERGENCIES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) may appear stable but can rapidly deteriorate due to overlapping hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and sepsis. Initial management should prioritize checking glucose and temperature, warming the child, and treating hypoglycemia promptly, as these steps can improve perfusion without aggressive fluid administration. Clinical signs of dehydration and shock are less reliable in SAM; hepatomegaly, weak pulses, and low urine output may indicate myocardial dysfunction alongside hypovolemia.
- **Clinical application:** For suspected shock in SAM, avoid routine large-volume fluid boluses; instead, administer 10 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid over 1 hour with close monitoring, repeating only if there is clear improvement and no signs of fluid overload. If there is no response to fluids, initiate vasoactive support and arrange urgent transfer rather than continuing fluid resuscitation. For dehydration without shock, oral or nasogastric rehydration using malnutrition-specific ORS formulations (e.g., ReSoMal) is preferred, with smaller, more frequent volumes and frequent reassessment. Early empiric antibiotic therapy is essential due to the high risk of occult bacteremia and pneumonia in SAM; reassess at 48–72 hours for possible de-escalation.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Electrolyte corrections should be performed cautiously, and micronutrients provided as per established protocols. Avoid diuretics unless pulmonary edema is confirmed and managed with specialist input. In India, early referral to a facility experienced in SAM management is crucial for cases of shock, severe respiratory distress, altered sensorium, or feed intolerance, as intensive monitoring is required and deterioration can be sudden. Close follow-up is essential to address ongoing risks of hypoglycemia, infection, and fluid balance complications.

case_studies.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition with Suspected Shock and Sepsis
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, poor feeding, and lethargy. His mother reports watery diarrhea for 2 days and reduced urine output. The child has a history of low weight gain and recurrent illnesses. On arrival, he appears emaciated with visible ribs, sunken eyes, and dry mucous membranes. His weight-for-height z-score is -4.5. He is hypothermic (rectal temperature 35.8°C), with a heart rate of 140 bpm, respiratory rate of 45/min, and capillary refill time of 4 seconds. Peripheral pulses are weak, and his extremities are cold. Hepatomegaly is noted on abdominal examination. There is no obvious respiratory distress, and oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. A bedside glucose check shows 52 mg/dL. The mother reports no history of vomiting, but the child has been too weak to drink oral rehydration solution at home.

subtopics.subtopic_title: UNDIFFERENTIATED ALTERED SENSORIUM FIRST HOUR COMA PATHWAY
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** A structured coma pathway is essential to reduce missed reversible causes of altered sensorium, particularly in Indian settings where infection, metabolic derangement, and poisoning frequently overlap. Initial management should prioritize ABCDE support and bedside glucose measurement, with treatment initiated alongside investigations rather than waiting for a definitive diagnosis. Early intervention targets the fastest killers, such as hypoglycemia, inadequate ventilation, seizures, and severe infections or toxidromes.
- **Clinical application:** If glucose is low or unknown, administer dextrose per age-appropriate protocols and recheck promptly, as hypoglycemia is a reversible cause of coma and seizures. For inadequate breathing, prioritize early ventilation or intubation over repeated sedative boluses. Treat seizures immediately per protocol, including escalation to second-line therapy if needed, particularly in cases of subtle signs like eye deviation or fluctuating consciousness. In unstable children with meningeal signs, petechiae, or shock, initiate empiric antimicrobials promptly after obtaining cultures, but do not delay antibiotics for lumbar puncture in such cases.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Defer lumbar puncture if raised intracranial pressure is suspected, indicated by progressive coma, focal deficits, papilledema, recurrent seizures, or severe cardiorespiratory compromise. Use neuroimaging selectively, ensuring it will influence management or safety decisions. In endemic areas, consider severe malaria when coma, recurrent seizures, anemia, jaundice, or hypoglycemia coexist, and initiate urgent testing and treatment for severe features without delay. For poisoning cases with cholinergic or opioid toxidromes, prioritize airway toileting, ventilation, and antidote administration based on clinical criteria.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CONVULSIVE STATUS EPILEPTICUS BENZODIAZEPINE AND SECOND LINE ANTISEIZURE PROTOCOL
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Convulsive status epilepticus is a time-critical emergency requiring immediate intervention to secure physiology and prevent hypoxic injury. Initial steps include ABCDE assessment, placing the child in a lateral position, administering oxygen, and checking bedside glucose to promptly treat hypoglycemia. Fever should be monitored and managed as it may act as a seizure trigger, and empiric antimicrobials should be started if meningitis or encephalitis is suspected.
- **Clinical application:** First-line therapy involves benzodiazepines, with buccal or intranasal midazolam at 0.2 mg/kg (maximum 10 mg) used when IV access is unavailable, and IV lorazepam at 0.1 mg/kg (maximum 4 mg) preferred when IV access is established. A second benzodiazepine dose can be administered if seizures persist after 5 minutes, but repeated doses should be avoided due to the risk of respiratory depression. Second-line antiseizure drugs include levetiracetam 40–60 mg/kg (maximum 3 g), fosphenytoin/phenytoin 20 mg/kg (phenytoin equivalents), or valproate 20–40 mg/kg, chosen based on availability and contraindications.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Refractory seizures necessitate escalation to ICU care for airway protection, continuous monitoring, and anesthetic infusions guided by protocol and, when available, EEG. Early referral is critical in India when second-line agents are required or airway support is anticipated, as transport delays can lead to hypoxic injury. Avoid excessive benzodiazepine dosing to minimize respiratory depression risks, and ensure timely intervention to prevent complications.

case_studies.vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department of a district hospital in rural India by his parents after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 15 minutes at home. subtopics.subtopic_title: FEBRILE SEIZURE CLASSIFICATION CNS INFECTION RED FLAGS AND DISCHARGE CRITERIA
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Febrile seizures are distinct from convulsive status epilepticus due to their typically short and self-limited nature. Classification is critical: simple febrile seizures are generalized, last <15 minutes, and occur once in 24 hours, while complex febrile seizures involve focal features, duration >15 minutes, or recurrence within 24 hours. The primary goal is to exclude CNS infection without unnecessary imaging or interventions.
- **Clinical application:** Stabilization is prioritized only if the seizure persists or recovery is delayed. For seizures lasting >5 minutes, use a single benzodiazepine strategy, such as buccal or intranasal midazolam, while monitoring for respiratory depression. Avoid repeated benzodiazepine doses to prevent hypoventilation. Post-seizure, reassess for CNS infection red flags, including altered sensorium, neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle, petechiae/purpura, shock signs, focal deficits, or disproportionate toxic appearance. If infection is suspected, stabilize, obtain cultures, initiate antibiotics as needed, and plan neuroimaging or lumbar puncture based on intracranial pressure risk.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Routine neuroimaging and EEG are unnecessary for simple febrile seizures. Discharge is appropriate when the child returns to baseline, vital signs stabilize, and there are no meningeal signs or persistent neurological abnormalities. Ensure the fever source is identified and caregivers receive clear written return precautions. In India, admission or extended observation is advised when follow-up reliability or travel barriers pose challenges.

case_studies.title: Febrile Seizure with CNS Infection Red Flag Assessment
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department of a district hospital in rural India after a generalized seizure lasting 4 minutes. The parents report he had a fever for the past day but no prior history of seizures. The child was postictal for 10 minutes but is now awake and irritable. On examination, his temperature is 39.2°C, heart rate 150/min, and capillary refill time is 2 seconds. No neck stiffness or bulging fontanelle is noted, but petechial spots are visible on his legs. He cries during the exam but has no focal neurological deficits. There is no history of rash, vomiting, or recent travel. The family lives in a remote village 40 km from the hospital, with limited access to follow-up care. A rapid malaria test is negative, and random blood glucose is 92 mg/dL. The parents are anxious and request immediate advice on the next steps.
case_studies.learning_objective: Classify febrile seizures, identify CNS infection red flags, and determine discharge criteria or admission need in a resource-limited setting.

case_studies.title: Febrile Seizure with CNS Infection Red Flags in a Rural Setting
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy is brought to a rural health center in India after a generalized seizure lasting approximately 7 minutes. The parents report that he had a fever for the past two days, treated intermittently with paracetamol. This was his first seizure episode. On arrival, he is drowsy but arousable, with a temperature of 39.2°C. His mother mentions that he has been less active and feeding poorly since morning. Examination reveals a bulging anterior fontanelle and petechial rash on his trunk. His capillary refill time is 4 seconds, and heart rate is 160 beats per minute. There are no focal neurological deficits, but he appears lethargic and irritable upon stimulation. The family lives in a remote village, 30 kilometers from the nearest hospital, with limited access to transportation or follow-up care. A rapid blood sugar test shows 92 mg/dL.
case_studies.learning_objective: Differentiate between simple and complex febrile seizures, identify CNS infection red flags, and apply discharge/admission criteria in a low-resource setting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ACUTE BACTERIAL MENINGITIS EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS AND LUMBAR PUNCTURE TIMING
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Acute bacterial meningitis should be suspected in any child presenting with fever and altered sensorium, neck stiffness, seizures, bulging fontanelle, petechiae, or signs of shock. Immediate stabilization of ABCDE, along with prompt treatment of seizures and hypoglycemia, is critical. Lumbar puncture (LP) is diagnostic but should be deferred in the presence of raised intracranial pressure or signs of impending herniation, such as progressive coma, focal neurological deficits, papilledema, recurrent seizures, or severe cardiorespiratory compromise.
- **Clinical application:** Empiric antibiotics should not be delayed for LP in unstable children; blood cultures should be obtained if LP is deferred. Antibiotic choice depends on age and local resistance patterns: ampicillin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., cefotaxime) is preferred for young infants to cover GBS and Listeria, while ceftriaxone is commonly used for older infants and children, with vancomycin added if cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus is a concern. Dexamethasone should be administered early, ideally before or with the first antibiotic dose, when Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) meningitis is suspected or as per local protocols. Routine neuroimaging before LP is unnecessary and should be reserved for high-risk features only when it will alter management.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Supportive care includes cautious fluid management, early treatment of shock, and monitoring for hyponatremia. In the Indian context, consider differential diagnoses such as tuberculosis, cerebral malaria, and viral encephalitis, particularly in subacute presentations or when focal deficits are observed. Daily reassessment is essential to refine the diagnosis and adjust therapy based on microbiological findings and clinical response. Escalation or de-escalation of therapy should be guided by evolving clinical and laboratory data.

case_studies.title: Acute Bacterial Meningitis in a Young Infant
case_studies.vignette: A 4-month-old male infant is brought to the emergency department in a rural hospital in India with a 2-day history of fever, irritability, and poor feeding. Over the past 6 hours, the parents report excessive drowsiness and a single episode of generalized tonic-clonic seizures lasting 2 minutes. On examination, the child is lethargic with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 9/15 (E2V2M5). His heart rate is 168/min, respiratory rate 48/min, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. He has a bulging anterior fontanelle and neck stiffness. Petechial spots are noted on the trunk. No focal neurological deficits are identified. Random blood glucose is 92 mg/dL. A bedside point-of-care lactate test shows 3.8 mmol/L. The local hospital lacks advanced imaging facilities, and the child appears hemodynamically unstable despite an initial fluid bolus.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize acute bacterial meningitis in a child presenting with fever, altered sensorium, and signs of raised intracranial pressure, and decide on the timing of lumbar puncture and initiation of empiric antibiotics in an unstable patient.

case_studies.title: Acute Bacterial Meningitis in a Febrile Infant
case_studies.vignette: A 6-month-old male infant is brought to a rural primary health center in India with a 2-day history of high-grade fever, irritability, and poor feeding. Over the last 6 hours, the mother reports the child has become increasingly drowsy and developed intermittent jerking movements involving both arms and legs lasting 1–2 minutes. On examination, the infant is febrile (39°C), lethargic, and has a bulging anterior fontanelle. His heart rate is 160/min, respiratory rate is 50/min, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. There are no petechial rashes or focal neurological deficits, but neck stiffness is noted. Random blood glucose is 62 mg/dL. The facility lacks advanced imaging or intensive care support. The nearest tertiary hospital is 3 hours away by road.

case_studies.title: Acute Bacterial Meningitis in a Febrile Infant with Altered Sensorium
case_studies.vignette: A 9-month-old male infant is brought to a rural primary health center in India with a 2-day history of high-grade fever, irritability, and poor feeding. Over the last 6 hours, the child has developed lethargy and two episodes of generalized seizures lasting 2–3 minutes each. On examination, the infant is febrile (39.2°C), drowsy, and has a bulging anterior fontanelle. His heart rate is 160/min, respiratory rate 48/min, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. No petechial rash is noted. Peripheral pulses are weak, and blood pressure is 65/40 mmHg. Neck stiffness is difficult to assess due to irritability. Point-of-care glucose is 48 mg/dL. The local facility lacks neuroimaging capabilities. The child’s parents report no history of trauma or recent travel. They cannot recall whether the child received all routine immunizations, including Hib vaccine.
case_studies.learning_objective: To recognize acute bacterial meningitis in a febrile child with altered sensorium and seizures, and to apply principles of empiric antibiotic initiation, lumbar puncture timing, and stabilization in a resource-limited setting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY RECOGNITION SECONDARY CAUSE SCREENING AND TITRATED REDUCTION
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Hypertensive emergencies in children are often under-recognized due to inconsistent BP measurement in distressed patients. They should be suspected in cases of severe hypertension with acute end-organ injury, including encephalopathy, seizures, severe headache with vomiting, papilledema, acute kidney injury, or pulmonary edema. Management priorities differ from isolated seizure treatment, focusing on controlled BP reduction and evaluation for secondary causes.
- **Clinical application:** Immediate actions include confirming BP readings with an appropriate cuff size and repeated measures while addressing pain and anxiety. Treatment should not be delayed in cases of encephalopathy, acute pulmonary edema, or severe renal failure. Secondary causes common in children, such as renal parenchymal disease, acute glomerulonephritis, renovascular disease, and endocrine disorders, should be rapidly screened using urine dipstick, creatinine, electrolytes, and targeted imaging when available.
- **Safety/follow-up:** BP reduction must be gradual to avoid cerebral or renal hypoperfusion, aiming for partial reduction initially with close neurologic monitoring. Once stable, stepwise reduction toward the child’s age and height target range is recommended. Preferred agents include titratable intravenous antihypertensives like labetalol or nicardipine, with continuous monitoring. In resource-limited settings, use the safest locally available regimen with frequent reassessment and prioritize early transfer for children with severe presentations such as encephalopathy, seizure, or pulmonary edema.

case_studies.title: Hypertensive Emergency in a 12-Year-Old with Severe Headache and Vomiting
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to the emergency department of a district hospital in rural India with a 2-day history of severe headache, intermittent vomiting, and lethargy. His parents report he has been increasingly irritable and confused over the past few hours. He has no prior history of hypertension or chronic illness. On examination, the child appears drowsy but responsive to painful stimuli. His blood pressure, measured with an appropriately sized cuff, is 190/120 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurements. Fundoscopic examination reveals blurred disc margins consistent with papilledema. His heart rate is 88 bpm, and respiratory rate is 22/min with no signs of respiratory distress. There is no significant edema, but urine dipstick testing reveals 2+ proteinuria. Initial point-of-care blood glucose is normal. No focal neurologic deficits are observed, but he complains of worsening headache when asked. The family denies recent trauma, fever, or toxin exposure, and there is no family history of hypertension or renal disease.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize hypertensive emergency in a pediatric patient and prioritize blood pressure confirmation, screening for secondary causes, and controlled BP reduction with close neurologic monitoring.

subtopics.subtopic_title: HYPOGLYCEMIA RECOGNITION DEXTROSE BOLUS OPTIONS AND PREVENTION OF RECURRENCE
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Hypoglycemia should be suspected and treated promptly in children presenting with seizures, altered sensorium, shock, or severe acute malnutrition (SAM). Symptoms can be non-specific, such as sweating, irritability, lethargy, or poor feeding, and may mimic sepsis. A blood glucose level ≤60 mg/dL (3.3 mmol/L) beyond the neonatal period warrants treatment, especially if symptomatic. In neonates, lower thresholds apply, and local neonatal protocols should guide management.
- **Clinical application:** Administer a dextrose bolus immediately when hypoglycemia is suspected, providing 0.5 g/kg glucose. Practical options include 5 mL/kg of 10% dextrose or 2 mL/kg of 25% dextrose via IV or IO access, followed by a flush. If only 50% dextrose is available, dilute it to avoid extravasation injury. If vascular access is delayed, give intramuscular glucagon if available, and initiate enteral feeds as soon as the airway is protected. Recheck blood glucose within 10–15 minutes and repeat treatment if necessary.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Prevent recurrence by starting a dextrose-containing maintenance infusion and providing frequent feeds once the child is stable. Investigate underlying causes such as sepsis, malaria, prolonged fasting, adrenal insufficiency, or insulin exposure. In children with SAM or severe infections, recurrent hypoglycemia is common; ensure scheduled feeding, provide nighttime feeds when possible, and avoid prolonged fasting. Document treatment times and perform repeat glucose checks to prevent missed episodes of recurrent hypoglycemia, which can lead to neurological injury.

case_studies.title: Hypoglycemia in a Malnourished Toddler with Seizures
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India is brought to the emergency department by his parents after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. The child has been lethargic and irritable for the past 2 days, with poor oral intake. He is known to have severe acute malnutrition (weight-for-height Z-score < -3) but has not been receiving regular nutritional rehabilitation. On arrival, he appears drowsy with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 10/15 (E3V2M5). His heart rate is 140 bpm, respiratory rate 30/min, and capillary refill time is 3 seconds. His extremities are cool, and he is diaphoretic. A bedside glucometer reading shows a blood glucose level of 40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L). No IV access has been established yet. The parents deny any known exposure to insulin or other medications.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize hypoglycemia in a child with severe acute malnutrition presenting with seizures and altered sensorium, initiate appropriate dextrose bolus therapy, and outline steps to prevent recurrence.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS CONTROLLED REHYDRATION INSULIN TIMING AND CEREBRAL INJURY SURVEILLANCE
subtopics.category: NEUROLOGIC AND METABOLIC EMERGENCIES WITH ALTERED SENSORIUM
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is characterized by hyperglycemia, ketonemia/ketonuria, and metabolic acidosis. Management includes controlled rehydration, insulin therapy, and electrolyte correction, with emphasis on avoiding rapid fluid shifts to reduce the risk of cerebral injury. Initial assessment should focus on identifying shock, altered mental status, and Kussmaul breathing, alongside obtaining baseline investigations such as electrolytes, urea/creatinine, venous blood gas, and bedside glucose without delaying stabilization.
- **Clinical application:** Administer a 10 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus only in cases of true shock (hypotension or markedly poor perfusion); routine large boluses are contraindicated due to increased cerebral injury risk. Initiate calculated rehydration with isotonic fluids (maintenance plus deficit replacement over 36–48 hours) and reassess clinically and with laboratory tests. Begin regular insulin infusion (0.05–0.1 units/kg/hour) after the first hour of fluids, ensuring potassium levels are known and urine output is established. Potassium replacement is critical, even if initial serum potassium is normal or high, and should be added to fluids once urine output is confirmed.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Monitor serum potassium and ECG frequently during treatment, as total-body potassium is depleted in DKA. Add dextrose to fluids when blood glucose levels drop to 250–300 mg/dL to allow continued insulin infusion for ketone clearance. Be vigilant for signs of cerebral injury, such as headache, vomiting, worsening sensorium, bradycardia, or rising blood pressure, and treat promptly with hypertonic saline 3% (5–10 mL/kg) or mannitol (0.5–1 g/kg) while reducing fluids and arranging ICU care. Avoid bicarbonate therapy unless specifically indicated, as it may worsen outcomes.

case_studies.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis in a 12-Year-Old with Altered Sensorium
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department in a semi-conscious state by her parents from a rural village. She has been unwell for three days with fever, abdominal pain, vomiting, and deep, labored breathing. Her parents report increased thirst and urination over the past week. On examination, she is lethargic with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 10/15. Her heart rate is 132 bpm, blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate is 36/min with Kussmaul breathing, and her capillary refill time is 3 seconds. She has dry mucous membranes and poor skin turgor. A bedside glucose test shows 450 mg/dL. Venous blood gas reveals pH 7.1 and bicarbonate 10 mmol/L. Serum potassium is 4.8 mmol/L. Urinalysis shows large ketones. There are no signs of focal neurological deficits, but her parents report that her drowsiness has worsened over the past few hours.
case_studies.learning_objective: Demonstrate understanding of controlled rehydration, insulin timing, and the importance of cerebral injury surveillance in pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis.

case_studies.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis in a 10-Year-Old: Balancing Rehydration and Monitoring for Cerebral Injury
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old girl presents to the emergency department of a district hospital in rural India with a 2-day history of vomiting, abdominal pain, and deep, labored breathing. Her parents report excessive thirst and weight loss over the past month. On examination, she is lethargic, with dry mucous membranes, delayed capillary refill of 4 seconds, and Kussmaul respirations. Her heart rate is 132 bpm, blood pressure is 88/60 mmHg, and she is afebrile. Bedside capillary glucose reads 'HIGH' on the glucometer. A venous blood gas reveals pH 7.08, HCO3- 6 mmol/L, and serum potassium 4.8 mmol/L. Urine dipstick shows large ketones. While fluids are initiated, she becomes intermittently irritable and complains of a headache. Her blood pressure rises to 110/70 mmHg, and her heart rate drops to 100 bpm. The team debates whether to escalate care locally or transfer her to a higher center.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the signs of cerebral injury during DKA management and understand the importance of controlled rehydration, insulin timing, and potassium monitoring in preventing complications.

case_studies.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis in a 10-Year-Old with Altered Sensorium
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old girl from a rural area presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of vomiting, abdominal pain, and rapid breathing. Her parents report increased thirst and frequent urination over the past week. On arrival, she is drowsy but arousable, with a heart rate of 128/min, respiratory rate of 36/min with deep Kussmaul breathing, blood pressure of 92/58 mmHg, and capillary refill time of 3 seconds. Her weight is 25 kg, and she appears dehydrated with dry mucous membranes. Point-of-care glucose is 450 mg/dL. The nurse notes a fruity odor on her breath. Initial venous blood gas shows pH 7.1 and bicarbonate 8 mmol/L. Serum potassium is 4.5 mmol/L. The family states that she was recently diagnosed with diabetes but has not been started on insulin due to financial constraints. No seizures or focal neurological signs are reported, but her drowsiness appears to be worsening during the evaluation.

case_studies.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis in a 10-Year-Old with Altered Sensorium
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of abdominal pain, vomiting, and deep breathing. Her parents report she has been excessively thirsty and urinating frequently for the past week. On examination, she appears drowsy and mildly confused. Her heart rate is 128 bpm, respiratory rate is 36/min with deep Kussmaul breathing, blood pressure is 92/58 mmHg, and capillary refill time is 3 seconds. Her skin is dry, and her mucous membranes are parched. A bedside glucose reading is 480 mg/dL. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.1 and bicarbonate 8 mmol/L. Serum potassium is 4.8 mmol/L. No urine output has been recorded since arrival. The family reports no prior history of diabetes. The emergency team begins initial stabilization while preparing to admit the patient for further management and monitoring.
case_studies.learning_objective: Explain the principles of controlled rehydration, insulin timing, potassium management, and cerebral injury surveillance in pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis.

case_studies.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis in a 12-Year-Old with Altered Sensorium
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural area presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of vomiting, abdominal pain, and lethargy. Her parents report excessive thirst and frequent urination for the past week. On arrival, she is drowsy but arousable, with deep, labored breathing. Vital signs show a heart rate of 120 bpm, respiratory rate of 32/min, blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg, and capillary refill time of 4 seconds. Her weight is 30 kg. Bedside glucose is 450 mg/dL. On examination, she has dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, and cool extremities. There is no focal neurological deficit, but she intermittently complains of a headache. Initial venous blood gas reveals a pH of 7.1 with a bicarbonate level of 8 mmol/L. Serum potassium is 5.2 mmol/L. Urinalysis shows large ketones. No urine output has been documented since arrival. While fluids are being prepared, she suddenly develops vomiting and becomes less responsive.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and manage diabetic ketoacidosis with emphasis on controlled rehydration, insulin timing, and vigilant monitoring for cerebral injury.

subtopics.subtopic_title: TOXIDROME RECOGNITION AIRWAY PROTECTION AND SAFE GASTROINTESTINAL DECONTAMINATION
subtopics.category: POISONING AND ENVENOMATION EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Pediatric poisoning in India commonly involves household chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and agricultural pesticides. Initial stabilization follows the ABCDE approach, with immediate management of hypoxia, seizures, and shock. A detailed history should include the agent, dose estimate, time of exposure, co-ingestants, and intent, particularly in adolescents where self-harm may be a concern. Physical examination focuses on toxidrome identification, such as cholinergic (salivation, lacrimation, bronchorrhea, bradycardia), opioid (miosis, respiratory depression), sympathomimetic (tachycardia, hypertension, agitation), and sedative-hypnotic (coma with hypoventilation).
- **Clinical application:** Activated charcoal (1 g/kg, maximum 50 g) is an option within 1 hour of a potentially toxic ingestion if the child is alert and has a protected airway, but it is not routine and is contraindicated when aspiration risk is high. Gastric lavage is rarely indicated and should only be considered for life-threatening ingestions presenting very early, and only if the airway is protected via intubation; routine lavage is harmful and discouraged. Charcoal is ineffective and contraindicated for hydrocarbons, corrosives, and heavy metals like iron. For hydrocarbon ingestion (e.g., kerosene), avoid inducing vomiting or lavage; instead, provide supportive care with oxygen and monitor for aspiration pneumonitis symptoms such as cough, tachypnea, or hypoxemia.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Airway protection is paramount in all decontamination decisions, with intubation required for high-risk procedures like gastric lavage. Early consultation with a poison information service improves safety, ensures appropriate antidote use, and aids documentation of timelines and evolving symptoms. Chest radiographs are reserved for symptomatic hydrocarbon ingestion cases, such as those presenting with cough, tachypnea, or hypoxemia. Clear documentation of clinical findings and management decisions is essential to avoid unnecessary interventions and ensure continuity of care.

case_studies.title: Toxidrome Recognition and Airway Protection in Pediatric Poisoning
case_studies.vignette: A 3-year-old boy from a rural village in India is brought to the emergency department by his parents after they found him playing near an open container of kerosene. They report he may have ingested an unknown quantity approximately 30 minutes ago. On arrival, the child is drowsy but arousable, with a strong smell of kerosene on his breath. He has a persistent cough and mild tachypnea with a respiratory rate of 34 breaths per minute. His oxygen saturation is 92% on room air. On examination, there are no signs of cyanosis, but auscultation reveals scattered coarse crepitations in both lung fields. His heart rate is 110 beats per minute, and blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. The parents deny any other ingestions or medications in the home. No vomiting has occurred since the exposure, but the child appears uncomfortable and intermittently restless.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ANTIDOTE PROTOCOLS FOR HIGH FREQUENCY POISONINGS AND TRANSFER THRESHOLDS
subtopics.category: POISONING AND ENVENOMATION EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Antidote protocols in pediatric poisonings should follow stabilization with ABCDE and glucose correction. Decontamination is airway-first: activated charcoal (1 g/kg, max 50 g) is indicated only for early toxic ingestions in alert children with protected airways, but is contraindicated for corrosives, hydrocarbons, or iron. Transfer thresholds must be clearly defined, with immediate transfer for persistent hypoxemia, shock requiring vasoactives, recurrent seizures, significant bronchorrhea, dysrhythmias, suspected esophageal button battery, or evolving corrosive airway injury.
- **Clinical application:** In organophosphate or carbamate poisoning (e.g., salivation, bronchorrhea, wheeze, bradycardia), prioritize suction, oxygenation, and early ventilation. Administer atropine 0.05 mg/kg IV or IO, titrated every 5 minutes to pulmonary endpoints (dry lungs, improved air entry), and continue as an infusion based on the effective loading dose. Add pralidoxime where available for confirmed organophosphate toxicity. For opioid toxicity (miosis, respiratory depression), ventilate and administer naloxone 0.1 mg/kg (max 2 mg) IV, IM, or intranasal, repeating as needed for ventilation; recurrent depression may require infusion and extended observation.
- **Safety/follow-up:** In tricyclic antidepressant toxicity with hypotension, seizures, or QRS widening on ECG, administer sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV, repeating until QRS narrows and perfusion improves, while correcting hypoxia and acidosis. Monitor for signs of antidote failure or complications, such as persistent bronchorrhea despite atropine or recurrent respiratory depression requiring naloxone infusion. Ensure timely transfer to higher care facilities for cases requiring antidote infusion, advanced airway management, or vasoactive support.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PARACETAMOL TOXICITY RISK STRATIFICATION NAC INFUSION AND SAFE DISCHARGE PLANNING
subtopics.category: POISONING AND ENVENOMATION EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Paracetamol overdose in children can present with mild or nonspecific early symptoms, making diagnosis challenging, especially in cases of staggered supratherapeutic dosing during febrile illnesses. Key history includes the formulation, dose in mg/kg, timing, and whether ingestion was single, staggered, or time-unknown. For single acute ingestions, a serum paracetamol concentration should be obtained at 4 hours post-ingestion (or as soon as possible thereafter) and interpreted using a treatment nomogram. Immediate initiation of NAC is indicated if the concentration is above the treatment line, the timing of ingestion is unknown but a potentially toxic dose is suspected, ingestion was staggered, or there are signs of liver injury.
- **Clinical application:** NAC infusion protocols must be carefully followed to ensure accurate dosing and minimize errors. A common two-bag regimen involves 200 mg/kg over 4 hours followed by 100 mg/kg over 16 hours (total 300 mg/kg), while a three-bag regimen includes 150 mg/kg over 1 hour, 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, and 100 mg/kg over 16 hours (total 300 mg/kg). Before starting infusion, use a checklist to confirm the child’s weight, concentration in the bag, infusion rate, and total mg to be delivered in each phase. Infusion reactions should be managed by pausing the infusion, administering antihistamines if needed, and restarting at a slower rate once the child is stable.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Do not discontinue NAC therapy blindly at 20–21 hours. Near the end of the infusion, reassess the child’s clinical status and liver function tests, including ALT, AST, bilirubin, and INR. NAC should be continued, and care escalated if transaminases are rising, INR is elevated, encephalopathy is present, or paracetamol remains detectable in the serum. Safe discharge requires that the child is asymptomatic, laboratory parameters are improving, reliable supervision is available, and caregivers are provided with clear return precautions.

subtopics.subtopic_title: VENOMOUS SNAKEBITE AND SCORPION STING EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT WITH EARLY REFERRAL
subtopics.category: POISONING AND ENVENOMATION EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Venomous snakebite and scorpion sting are pediatric emergencies in India requiring rapid supportive care and early referral decisions. Snakebite management includes limb immobilization, avoiding tourniquets, cutting, or suction, and conducting a 20-minute whole blood clotting test (20WBCT) using a clean, dry glass tube. Scorpion envenomation often presents with an autonomic storm (sweating, tachycardia, cold extremities) and can progress to pulmonary edema or cardiogenic shock.
- **Clinical application:** Administer anti-snake venom (ASV) for systemic envenomation signs such as neurotoxicity, coagulopathy (non-clotting 20WBCT), spontaneous bleeding, or rapidly progressive swelling. Initial ASV doses should follow protocol, with reassessment and repeat 20WBCT guiding further doses; persistent coagulopathy typically requires additional ASV rather than plasma. For scorpion stings, early prazosin (30 micrograms/kg orally every 3–6 hours) is critical until extremities are warm and perfusion improves. Ventilatory support is essential for neurotoxic snakebites, and inotropes like dobutamine are indicated for myocardial dysfunction in severe scorpion envenomation.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Be vigilant for anaphylaxis during ASV infusion and manage promptly with IM epinephrine and supportive care. Monitor airway and breathing closely in snakebite cases to detect evolving neuroparalysis, and do not delay referral if respiratory weakness develops. Severe scorpion envenomation cases require high-dependency or ICU monitoring, with early transfer if ventilation or inotropes are needed. Shock in scorpion stings should be treated cautiously, with oxygen and tailored fluid resuscitation to prevent exacerbating pulmonary edema.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ACUTE WATERY DIARRHEA DEHYDRATION CLASSIFICATION AND ORS OR IV REHYDRATION PLANS
subtopics.category: ACUTE DEHYDRATION AND BURNS EMERGENCY STABILIZATION
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Acute watery diarrhea is a leading cause of pediatric dehydration and preventable deaths. Dehydration is classified clinically into no dehydration, some dehydration, or severe dehydration based on mental status, thirst, sunken eyes, skin pinch, and perfusion. Hypoglycemia and hypothermia should always be assessed in infants and malnourished children, with shock requiring immediate treatment. Zinc supplementation for 10–14 days is recommended, and unnecessary antiemetics or antidiarrheals should be avoided.
- **Clinical application:** For no dehydration, provide extra fluids at home, continue feeding, and breastfeeding. For some dehydration, administer ORS at 75 mL/kg over 4 hours using small frequent sips or nasogastric routes if needed, reassess hydration status, and continue ORS after each stool. Severe dehydration or shock requires IV isotonic fluids, typically 30 mL/kg rapidly followed by 70 mL/kg over 2.5–5 hours depending on age, with frequent reassessment and an early switch back to oral rehydration once perfusion improves. Children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) need slower rehydration with close monitoring for fluid overload.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Caregivers should be counseled to use safe water, ensure correct ORS preparation, and seek prompt medical attention if the child exhibits inability to drink, persistent vomiting, blood in stool, worsening lethargy, or reduced urine output. Reassess hydration status after the initial rehydration period for signs such as ongoing tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill, and reduced urine output, and repeat oral rehydration if necessary. Antibiotics are reserved for dysentery, suspected cholera with severe dehydration, or specific bacterial infections. In India, emphasis should be placed on caregiver education to prevent recurrence and complications.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PEDIATRIC BURN RESUSCITATION FLUID TITRATION AND INHALATION INJURY AIRWAY DECISIONS
subtopics.category: ACUTE DEHYDRATION AND BURNS EMERGENCY STABILIZATION
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Initial burn management prioritizes airway, breathing, and circulation, with early recognition of inhalation injury and shock. Inhalation injury should be suspected in cases of facial burns, singed nasal hair, soot in the mouth, hoarseness, stridor, wheeze, or enclosed-space exposure. Early airway securing is critical when progressive edema is anticipated, and high-flow oxygen is mandatory if carbon monoxide exposure is possible. Burn size should be estimated using %TBSA (excluding simple erythema) to guide fluid resuscitation and referral, with frequent reassessment as early estimates are often inaccurate.
- **Clinical application:** Fluid resuscitation for major burns should be initiated and titrated to endpoints like perfusion and urine output (target ~1 mL/kg/hour in children, higher in small infants). Common formulas include Parkland-style (3–4 mL/kg/%TBSA) or modified Brooke-style (2 mL/kg/%TBSA) for the first 24 hours, plus maintenance fluids. Administer half the calculated resuscitation volume in the first 8 hours from the time of burn and the remainder over the next 16 hours, using isotonic crystalloid. In young children, dextrose-containing maintenance fluids should be added to mitigate hypoglycemia risk, and local burn protocols must be followed with documentation of the chosen formula.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Fluids should be titrated based on clinical endpoints, with close monitoring of respiratory effort, lung auscultation, and weight to avoid fluid overload, which worsens outcomes. Early transfer is indicated for large %TBSA burns, suspected inhalation injury, electrical or chemical burns, circumferential limb or chest burns, or burns involving critical areas like the face, hands, feet, genitalia, or major joints. Referral is also essential when critical care monitoring is unavailable. Continuous reassessment of burn size, perfusion, and respiratory status is crucial to guide ongoing management and escalation.

case_studies.title: Pediatric Burn Resuscitation and Airway Assessment in a 5-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department in a semi-urban hospital in India after a house fire in an enclosed kitchen. The incident occurred approximately 2 hours ago. On arrival, he is alert but visibly distressed, with soot around his mouth and nostrils, singed nasal hairs, and superficial burns over his face, neck, and anterior trunk. His parents report that he was trapped in the kitchen for several minutes before being rescued. He has a persistent hoarse voice and occasional coughing but no stridor at rest. His respiratory rate is 36/min with mild suprasternal retractions, and oxygen saturation on room air is 91%. On examination, the burned areas are erythematous with some blistering, estimated to involve 18% total body surface area (TBSA). Capillary refill is 3 seconds, and his heart rate is 140/min. No urine output has been recorded since the incident. The local facility has limited resources, but the child has been placed on high-flow oxygen. The team is preparing for fluid resuscitation and considering transfer to a higher center.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize signs of inhalation injury and determine priorities for airway management and fluid resuscitation in pediatric burn emergencies.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PEDIATRIC TRAUMA PRIMARY SURVEY HEMORRHAGE CONTROL AND FRACTURE STABILIZATION
subtopics.category: TRAUMA INJURY AND POST EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Trauma care in children must follow a structured approach to address preventable causes of death, such as missed airway and hemorrhage issues. The ABCDE primary survey, including cervical spine protection, is essential, with life-threatening conditions treated as they are identified. Early markers of hemorrhagic shock in children include tachycardia, cool extremities, and weak pulses, as they can maintain blood pressure until late stages. A restrictive crystalloid approach is advised, with 10 mL/kg aliquots used only while preparing blood products and arranging urgent transfer.
- **Clinical application:** Control external bleeding with direct pressure and splint long-bone fractures promptly to prevent further injury and hypothermia. Suspected fractures and dislocations should be immobilized, with neurovascular checks repeated to monitor for compartment syndrome (signs include pain out of proportion, paresthesia, pallor, pulselessness, and paralysis). Analgesia is critical to improve cooperation and reduce sympathetic stress but should be used cautiously in unstable patients to avoid excessive sedation. Non-accidental injury must be considered in cases with inconsistent history, delayed presentation, patterned bruising, or multiple injuries of varying ages.
- **Safety/follow-up:** In cases of suspected non-accidental injury, immediate actions include ensuring the child’s safety, treating injuries, and documenting findings meticulously, including photographs if permitted by policy. In India, early multidisciplinary escalation and clear documentation are crucial due to high social risk and potential challenges in follow-up. Early blood transfusion should be prioritized in ongoing shock when available. Ensure adherence to local protocols, involving child protection and senior teams as necessary to safeguard the child and ensure continuity of care.
